title: Dog Dragging Hind Feet: Causes, Urgency, and Next Steps
meta_desc: If your dog is dragging their back feet, it may signal nerve or spine issues. Learn about urgency levels and how to help your pet today.
content_html:
TL;DR: Hind foot dragging (knuckling) in dogs is typically a sign of a neurological or spinal issue that requires a veterinary evaluation within 24 to 48 hours. Providing a video of your dog walking at home can help your vet quickly assess the severity and prioritize care.

What does it mean if my dog is dragging its hind feet?

Dragging the hind feet, often referred to by veterinarians as knuckling or scuffing, occurs when a dog lacks the coordination or strength to lift their paws properly while walking. You might notice a scraping sound on the pavement or see wear on the tops of your dog's nails. This is typically a sign that the communication between the brain and the limbs is being disrupted, often due to issues in the spine or nervous system.

Is it an emergency if my dog starts dragging its back legs?

  • The urgency level for hind foot dragging is Medium.
  • Seek a veterinary evaluation within 24 to 48 hours to prevent permanent nerve damage.
  • If the dragging is accompanied by a total inability to walk or signs of extreme pain, treat it as an immediate emergency.

How can photos or videos help the veterinary team triage my dog?

  • Capture a video of your dog walking on a flat surface to show their natural gait outside of the clinic environment.
  • Take clear photos of scuffed nails or skin to document physical wear.
  • Provide these visuals to help the triage team accurately assess neurological deficits and prioritize your pet's care.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Dragging of the hind feet in dogs can be caused by peripheral nerve injuries, commonly affecting the sciatic nerve or its branches due to pelvic fractures, intramedullary pin placement in the femur, or irritating injections near the nerve. With nerve root avulsion, the dog may bear little or no weight on the limb and drag the dorsal surface of the paw, potentially leading to self-mutilation requiring amputation; prognosis is poor with complete avulsion, though recovery is possible in mild contusion cases. A short-strided gait in the pelvic limbs progressing to flaccid tetraparesis or tetraplegia can also be indicative of other conditions such as polyradiculoneuritis.

Chapter: Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1244)

faqs.question: What are the most common causes of foot dragging?
faqs.answer: Common causes include Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD), Degenerative Myelopathy, spinal arthritis, or previous injuries to the back.

faqs.question: What should I do while waiting for my vet appointment?
faqs.answer: Strictly limit your dog's activity. Avoid stairs, jumping on furniture, and long walks. Use a harness or a sling under the belly to assist them if they are unstable.

faqs.question: Can this be cured?
faqs.answer: The prognosis depends on the underlying cause. Some conditions respond well to anti-inflammatory medication and rest, while others may require physical therapy or surgery.
